perturbation.original_text: 3. Exclusions. Confidential Information does not include, or shall cease to include, information or material that the Recipient can demonstrate by then- contemporaneous written records: (i) is in the Recipient’s possession at the time of disclosure as shown by the Recipient’s files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (ii) before or after it has been disclosed to Recipient, becomes part of public knowledge or literature, not as a result of any action or inaction of the Recipient; (iii) is approved for release by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (iv) is disclosed to the R ecipient by a third party not in violation of any obligation of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by the Recipient without reference to Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Exclusions. Confidential Information does not include, or shall cease to include, information or material that the Recipient can demonstrate by any records: (i) is in the Recipient’s possession at the time of disclosure as shown by the Recipient’s files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure; (ii) before or after it has been disclosed to Recipient, becomes part of public knowledge or literature, not as a result of any action or inaction of the Recipient; (iii) is approved for release by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (iv) is disclosed to the R ecipient by a third party not in violation of any obligation of confidentiality; or (v) is independently developed by the Recipient without reference to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'then-contemporaneous written records' has been changed to 'any records' which introduces an in-text contradiction. The new clause in section 3 now allows for the Recipient to demonstrate that the information is not confidential through any records and not just 'then-contemporaneous written records', this creates an in-text contradiction regarding the requirements in order to show the information is not confidential. This new change, creates a contradiction in section 9 of the document, in regards to remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. Remedies. The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. T he Recipient acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its promises or agreements contained herein will result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including specific performance and injunctive relief, in the event of any breach or threatened breach or intended breach of t his Agreement by the Recipient. Such remedies, however, shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for any breach of the Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 3 modifies the criteria for excluding information from being considered confidential. The original text requires 'then-contemporaneous written records,' while the changed text broadens this to 'any records.' This directly contradicts the assumptions in section 9, which states that the Disclosing Party is relying on the agreement and the Recipient will be responsible for damages caused by a breach of the agreement. By allowing 'any records' instead of 'then-contemporaneous written records,' the changed text weakens the Disclosing Party's protection and creates a contradiction regarding the requirements in order to show the information is not confidential which is a key consideration for remedy, creating a potential loophole, and making it unclear how section 9 should be enforced, creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the provided definition of inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Use Limitations. The Recipient (i) agrees not to use the Confidential Information for its own use or for any purposes except the Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above; (ii) shall not use the Confidential Information for purposes of unfair or improper competition; and (iii) agrees not to copy, alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any of the materials unless permitted in writing by the Disclosing Party (any copies that are authorized to be made will be identified as belonging to th e Disclosing Party and marked as “confidential”, “proprietary” or with similar legend). Nothing in this Agreement shall limit or restrict the rights of the Disclosing Party to assert infringement or other intellectual property claims against the Recipien t or to impose on either party any obligation to disclose any Confident ial Information, to purchase or sell any products, or to otherwise enter in to any type of business relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Use Limitations. The Recipient (i) agrees to use the Confidential Information for its own use, even outside of the Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above; (ii) shall not use the Confidential Information for purposes of unfair or improper competition; and (iii) agrees not to copy, alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any of the materials unless permitted in writing by the Disclosing Party (any copies that are authorized to be made will be identified as belonging to th e Disclosing Party and marked as “confidential”, “proprietary” or with similar legend). Nothing in this Agreement shall limit or restrict the rights of the Disclosing Party to assert infringement or other intellectual property claims against the Recipien t or to impose on either party any obligation to disclose any Confident ial Information, to purchase or sell any products, or to otherwise enter in to any type of business relationship.
perturbation.explanation: The change in section 4 introduces an in-text contradiction by allowing the Recipient to use the Confidential Information for its own use, even outside of the Authorized Purpose expressly set forth above, which directly contradicts the original clause in section 4(i). This contradiction creates uncertainty about the Recipient's obligations, impacting clause 1 in Section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Purpose. Each of the Company and Supplier desires to receive Confidential Information (as defined below) of the other for the purpose of con sidering a commercial transaction and/or ongoing business relationship with the other (the “ Authorized Purpose ”). For purposes of this Agreement, the party disclosing Confidential Information is hereinafter referred to as the “ Disclosing Party ” and the pa rty receiving Confidential Information is hereinafter referred to as the “ Recipient ”. In connection with the Authorized Purpose, certain trade secrets and business information proprietary to the Disclosing Party, and which the Disclosing Party considers confidential, may be provided to the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4(i) explicitly allows the Recipient to use Confidential Information outside of the Authorized Purpose, directly contradicting the definition of 'Authorized Purpose' in Section 1. Section 1 defines 'Authorized Purpose' as considering a commercial transaction and/or ongoing business relationship. The modified Section 4(i) negates this defined purpose by allowing use even outside of it, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Non -Disclosure. The Recipient agrees not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third parties or to any of its employees, dire ctors, officers, agents, contractors, consultants, advisors, or other representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”), except those Representatives who have a need to know the Confidential Information for accomplishing the Authorized Purpose described her ein. In such instance(s), the Representatives (i) shall be made aware that the Confidential Information is confidential; and (ii) that such Representatives’ disclosure of the Confidential Information is restricted as set forth herein. Notwithsta nding the foregoing, the Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the extent required by a valid order of a court of competent jurisdiction or other governmental body, or by applicable law; provided, however, that the Recipient will use all reasonable efforts to promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the obligation to make such disclosure in advance of the disclosure, unless otherwise prohibited by law or court order, so that the Disclosing Party will have a reasonable opportunity to object to such disclosure. The Recipient and its Representatives shall cooperate in all reasonable respects with the Disclosing Party in seeking to prevent or limit disclosure; and, in the event that a protective order or other remedy is not obtained, t he Recipient will limit disclosure to the Confidential Information actually required to be disclosed, provided the Recipient exercises its reasonable efforts to obtain reasonable assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to the Disclosing Par ty’s Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees that it shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature; provided that in no event shall the R ecipient exercise less than reasonable care to protect the Confidential Information commensurate with the sensitivity of such information. The Recipient agrees to advise the SUPPLIER MUTUAL CONFIDENTIAL NON- DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT SNDA-1 Rev D Page 2 of 3 Disclosing Party in writing of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information of which the Recipient may become aware. The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that certain Confidential Information will be subject to export restrictions under the Arms Export Control Act (22 U.S.C. 2751 et seq.) or Executive Order 12470. In such instances, in addition to general non -disclosure restrictions set forth above, such Confidential Information shall not be transferred to any foreign person in the United States or abroad, except where authorized by the United States Department of State (ITAR) or the United States Department of Commerce (EAR). Such Confidential Information shall be appropriately marked and identified at the time of transfer.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Non -Disclosure. The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to any third parties or to any of its employees, dire ctors, officers, agents, contractors, consultants, advisors, or other representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”), regardless of whether those Representatives have a need to know the Confidential Information for accomplishing the Authorized Purpose described her ein. In such instance(s), the Representatives (i) shall not be made aware that the Confidential Information is confidential; and (ii) that such Representatives’ disclosure of the Confidential Information is not restricted as set forth herein. Notwithsta nding the foregoing, the Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the extent required by a valid order of a court of competent jurisdiction or other governmental body, or by applicable law; provided, however, that the Recipient will use all reasonable efforts to promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the obligation to make such disclosure in advance of the disclosure, unless otherwise prohibited by law or court order, so that the Disclosing Party will have a reasonable opportunity to object to such disclosure. The Recipient and its Representatives shall cooperate in all reasonable respects with the Disclosing Party in seeking to prevent or limit disclosure; and, in the event that a protective order or other remedy is not obtained, t he Recipient will limit disclosure to the Confidential Information actually required to be disclosed, provided the Recipient exercises its reasonable efforts to obtain reasonable assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to the Disclosing Par ty’s Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees that it shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature; provided that in no event shall the R ecipient exercise less than reasonable care to protect the Confidential Information commensurate with the sensitivity of such information. The Recipient agrees to advise the SUPPLIER MUTUAL CONFIDENTIAL NON- DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT SNDA-1 Rev D Page 2 of 3 Disclosing Party in writing of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information of which the Recipient may become aware. The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that certain Confidential Information will be subject to export restrictions under the Arms Export Control Act (22 U.S.C. 2751 et seq.) or Executive Order 12470. In such instances, in addition to general non -disclosure restrictions set forth above, such Confidential Information shall not be transferred to any foreign person in the United States or abroad, except where authorized by the United States Department of State (ITAR) or the United States Department of Commerce (EAR). Such Confidential Information shall be appropriately marked and identified at the time of transfer.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 5 introduces an in-text contradiction. The modified text now states that the Recipient *may* disclose Confidential Information to any third party, even if those Representatives do not have a need to know the Confidential Information for accomplishing the Authorized Purpose. This directly contradicts the original restriction in Section 5, in the first sentence, that limits disclosure to only those representatives who 'have a need to know'. Additionally, now it states that the Representatives should not be made aware of the confidential information, contradicting with original statement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non -Disclosure. The Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information to any third parties or to any of its employees, dire ctors, officers, agents, contractors, consultants, advisors, or other representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”), regardless of whether those Representatives have a need to know the Confidential Information for accomplishing the Authorized Purpose described her ein. In such instance(s), the Representatives (i) shall not be made aware that the Confidential Information is confidential; and (ii) that such Representatives’ disclosure of the Confidential Information is not restricted as set forth herein. The Recipient and its Representatives shall cooperate in all reasonable respects with the Disclosing Party in seeking to prevent or limit disclosure; and, in the event that a protective order or other remedy is not obtained, t he Recipient will limit disclosure to the Confidential Information actually required to be disclosed, provided the Recipient exercises its reasonable efforts to obtain reasonable assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded to the Disclosing Par ty’s Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees that it shall treat the Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature; provided that in no event shall the R ecipient exercise less than reasonable care to protect the Confidential Information commensurate with the sensitivity of such information. The Recipient agrees to advise the SUPPLIER MUTUAL CONFIDENTIAL NON- DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT SNDA-1 Rev D Page 2 of 3 Disclosing Party in writing of any misappropriation or misuse by any person of such Confidential Information of which the Recipient may become aware. The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that certain Confidential Information will be subject to export restrictions under the Arms Export Control Act (22 U.S.C. 2751 et seq.) or Executive Order 12470. Such Confidential Information shall be appropriately marked and identified at the time of transfer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 directly contradicts the original intent of the Non-Disclosure agreement, as described in the explanation. The original text restricted disclosure, while the changed text permits disclosure to any third party regardless of need, and also contradicts that the representatives should not be made aware that the confidential information is confidential, which contradicts the original statement of the section. The contradicted text is section 5, and by the Perturbation Explantion given, it is clear that the change leads to a contradiction, because the recipient *may* disclose the confidential information to any third party, regardless of need, contradicting the need-to-know basis established earlier in the section.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of the Disclosing Party that are furnished to the Recipient, and all copies thereof, at the earlier of the Disclosing Party’s request for return of the materials, or the termination of the business relationship between the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, at the Disclosing Party’s option, will either be (subject to the Disclosing P arty’s reasonable instructions): (i) promptl y returned to the Disclosing Party and/or (ii) destroyed by the Recipient, including materials stored on the Recipient’s computing environment, or electronic storage media, to the extent technically feasible (with the Recipient providing written certification of such destruction).
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Return of Materials. Any materials or documents of the Disclosing Party that are furnished to the Recipient, and all copies thereof, at the earlier of the Disclosing Party’s request for return of the materials, or the termination of the business relationship between the Disclosing Party and the Recipient, at the Recipient's option, will either be (subject to the Disclosing P arty’s reasonable instructions): (i) promptl y returned to the Disclosing Party and/or (ii) destroyed by the Recipient, including materials stored on the Recipient’s computing environment, or electronic storage media, to the extent technically feasible (with the Recipient providing written certification of such destruction).
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 7 introduces an in-text contradiction by stating the materials will be returned or destroyed at the Recipient's option, instead of the Disclosing Party's option. This contradicts Section 8, which states that 'The Disclosing Party shall retain the sole ownership and right to possess its Confidential Information.' This creates ambiguity about the ownership of the materials.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. No License. The Disclosing Party shall retain the sole ownership and right to possess its Confidential Information, and any derivatives thereof. No license is granted to the Recipient under any patents, copyrights, mask w ork rights or other proprietary rights by the disclosure of any information hereunder, nor is any warranty made as to such information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 7, giving the Recipient the option to return or destroy confidential information. This directly contradicts Section 8, which states that the Disclosing Party retains sole ownership and the right to possess its Confidential Information. The Recipient's option to destroy the information conflicts with the Disclosing Party's right to possess it, creating an inconsistency as defined by the prompt.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations und er this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or for a period of five (5) years after the dat e of disclos ure, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations und er this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or forever.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction in section 12 regarding the termination period of the Confidential Information. It was originally stated that the Recipient’s obligations would continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or for a period of five (5) years after the date of disclosure, whichever is later, however the changed text states that it will be forever. The new statement makes it unclear how long the Recipient is actually held responsible for not sharing the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. Term; Termination. This Agreement shall govern all communications among the parties that are made from the date Confidential Inf ormation was first provided to or obtained by the Recipient (even if prior to the Effective Date) through the date on which a party receives from the other party written notice that such party is terminating this Agreement. The Recipient’s obligations und er this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information it has received or obtained prior to such termination shall continue until as long as the Confidential Information remains a trade secret or forever.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 12 modifies the duration of the Recipient's obligations regarding Confidential Information. The original text states that the obligations continue until the Confidential Information is no longer a trade secret or for five years, whichever is later. The changed text extends this period to 'forever'. This directly contradicts the previous limitation, creating an in-text contradiction within section 12 itself. The contradiction arises because the two statements regarding the duration of confidentiality obligations are incompatible, as an obligation lasting forever is inherently different from one lasting for a limited time (either until the information is no longer a trade secret or for five years).
